retrieved_docs.title: Bruxism Management -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Intermittent bruxism is prevalent, particularly among children. Often, patients remain asymptomatic, and treatment is not warranted. [17] Typically, clinicians treat bruxism when adverse effects on oral structures occur or in the presence of concurrent conditions such as neurological or sleep disorders or those listed below. Mechanical wear of the teeth causing the loss of occlusal morphology and flattening of the occlusal surfaces Hypersensitive teeth Fractured teeth Fractures of restorations, crowns, and partial prosthesis Failure of dental implants Masticatory muscle hypertrophy Tenderness and stiffness in jaw muscles Restricted opening of the mouth Temporomandibular joint pain Preauricular pain Clicking and tenderness of the temporomandibular joint Headaches due to temporalis muscle tightness and tenderness Unpleasant loud noises during sleep that cause sleep disturbances [18] [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Cracked Tooth Syndrome -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: A minor high spot may result in occlusal trauma when a patient has undergone recent dental treatment. In addition, it is advisable to assess static and dynamic occlusion using articulating paper and adjust the restoration if indicated. Galvanic pain should also be considered as a differential diagnosis, especially if a patient has had a recent restoration placed. In addition to the above, clinicians should consider orofacial pain as part of the differential diagnosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Occlusal Contact Relations And Mandibular Movements -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: There are many ways to examine the quality of occlusion, and this is exemplified when considering dental implants or denture design. In maxillofacial trauma, occlusion can be altered from the trauma itself or suboptimal management of fractures. Keeping the dental occlusion as the foundation for these repairs prevents long-term malocclusion. [3] In other cases, malocclusion can be developmental and adversely impact cosmesis, which is the case for some patients seeking orthognathic surgery. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Mastication Muscles -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Myofascial pain and dysfunction may result from several etiologies. The most common ones are nocturnal bruxism, habitual clenching of the mouth, and whiplash injuries during a trauma. Temporomandibular joint (TMJ) dysfunction can result from an imbalance of forces within the muscles of mastication.  Grinding of teeth at night (bruxism) is a common cause of TMJ dysfunction secondary to a resultant imbalance in the muscle of mastication forces from excessive grinding of the teeth. [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma to the Primary Dentition -- History and Physical -- Extrusive Luxation
retrieved_docs.content: The partial displacement of the tooth out of its socket is known as extrusive luxation. The tooth appears elongated and has increased mobility. Occlusal interference may be present. A radiographic examination is recommended.

retrieved_docs.title: Otalgia -- Differential Diagnosis -- Possible Reasons for Secondary Otalgia
retrieved_docs.content: TMJ condition: TMJ dysfunction, bruxism

retrieved_docs.title: Mandible Body Fracture -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: In general, the common signs of a body fracture include laceration in the gingiva, step defect in the occlusion, and/or ecchymosis in the floor of the mouth. Ecchymosis in the mouth floor is a pathognomonic sign of a mandibular body fracture. The examiner should note the mobility in the fracture. To do so, use both hands to manipulate the mandible by placing the thumb on the occlusal surface of the teeth and fingers on the inferior border of the mandible. Then slowly and carefully, pressure should be placed between the two hands. [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma to the Primary Dentition -- History and Physical -- Lateral Luxation
retrieved_docs.content: The displacement of the tooth in a palatal/lingual or labial direction is known as lateral luxation. The tooth will be immobile and occlusal interference may be present. A radiographic examination is recommended.

retrieved_docs.title: Physical and Chemical Lesions of the Oral Mucosa -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Physical and chemical lesions of the oral cavity are frequently identified during routine examinations. Common causes include continuous exposure to trauma, chemical agents, traumatic occlusion, sharp occlusal anatomy, acidic and alkaline substances, medications, and food or beverages at varying temperatures. Depending on the nature and extent of the insult, the lesions may present with symptoms such as pain, ulceration, erythema, or swelling.

retrieved_docs.title: Trismus -- Etiology -- Trismus As a complication of Trauma to the Face and Mandible
retrieved_docs.content: Fractures, mainly mandibular, may result in limited mouth opening. [1] Although rare, fractures of the zygomatic arch and zygomaticomaxillary complex can cause trismus as they impede the movement of the coronoid process. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Mandible Fracture -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Patients will present with mandibular pain, facial asymmetry, deformity, and dysphagia. Malocclusion, a decreased range of movement of the temporomandibular joint, trismus, or lower lip numbness can also be present. Determining the mechanism of injury is important. In a motor vehicle accident, the patient usually suffers from multiple, compound, or communicated mandibular fractures. Mandibular fracture from an altercation is more likely to be single, simple, and nondisplaced.

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma to the Primary Dentition -- Treatment / Management -- Lateral Luxation Injuries
retrieved_docs.content: If there is minimal or no occlusal interference, the tooth should be allowed to reposition spontaneously, usually within six months. If the tooth is severely displaced and there is a risk of ingestion or aspiration, the tooth should be extracted under local anesthesia.

retrieved_docs.title: Bruxism Management -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Potential complications of bruxism treatment include malocclusion, dental carries due to improper dental hygiene, temporomandibular disorders, and teeth staining. In addition, some occlusal devices only cover the anterior teeth. When the posterior teeth erupt in children wearing this type of device, they can develop an anterior open bite where a lack of overlap or contact between maxillary and mandibular incisors exists, while the posterior teeth are in occlusion. This development leads to unnecessary orthodontic interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma to the Primary Dentition -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Clinicians should assess whether the injuries sustained correlate and are consistent with the history of the accident. Repeated injuries, multiple bruises, delayed presentation, or injuries with unclear explanations may raise suspicions of abuse. [9] In scenarios where there is suspicion of abuse, prompt referral following local safeguarding protocols should be followed to investigate the incident thoroughly.

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma to the Primary Dentition -- Treatment / Management -- Root Fractures
retrieved_docs.content: If the coronal fragment is not displaced, no treatment is required. If the coronal fragment is displaced and is not excessively mobile, leave the coronal fragment to reposition spontaneously, even if occlusal interference is present. A clinical follow-up examination is recommended after one week, six to eight weeks, and one year. Continue clinical follow-up yearly until the eruption of the permanent successor.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Occlusal Contact Relations And Mandibular Movements -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Occlusion is the articulation between the mandibular and maxillary dental arch. Static and dynamic occlusal relationships can be described in many ways. [3] [4] Understanding the occlusal contact relationship impacts the providers' ability to design and place dental implants, implement orthodontic braces, fit dentures, re-establish masticatory function, and restore a patient’s native bite after maxillofacial trauma.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 797
retrieved_docs.content: particular bones) are nested under a universal category of late effects, which also encompasses non‑fracture injuries such as burns and frostbite. Recognizing these relationships aids clinicians in systematically assessing and managing the long‑term sequelae of diverse traumatic injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2559
retrieved_docs.content: The phrase “Due to” is associated with crushing injury or internal injury, indicating that these types of injuries are frequently described as occurring because of specific causative events. This association highlights the importance of identifying the underlying mechanisms when evaluating patients with crushing or internal injuries, as the wording “due to” often signals a direct link between the injury and its precipitating factor.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1781
retrieved_docs.content: Crushing injury or internal injury is associated with the medical condition referred to as إصابة (injury), indicating that such traumatic forces commonly result in injury.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 256
retrieved_docs.content: diverse injury types (intracranial, skeletal, facial), their specific anatomical subcategories, corresponding treatment modalities, and long‑term sequelae are interrelated, while also incorporating psychosocial determinants such as aggression that may influence injury occurrence and outcome. This integrated framework supports comprehensive assessment, classification, and management of patients presenting with complex traumatic or toxic presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 1057
retrieved_docs.content: grouped under a unified late‑effect classification that is further subdivided by anatomical region. This structured relationship aids clinicians in linking acute injury diagnoses with their potential long‑term sequelae and facilitates comprehensive patient management across different injury types and body regions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2082
retrieved_docs.content: Crushing injuries or internal injuries are associated with open wounds (جرح مفتوح), indicating that such traumatic forces often result in visible tissue disruption.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1184
retrieved_docs.content: effects as specific conditions associated with the concept of “كسر,” situates them within a broader category of unspecified injury sequelae, and further delineates limb‑specific fracture sequelae, thereby providing a structured framework for understanding and managing the long‑term consequences of various bone injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2454
retrieved_docs.content: FACE INJURY TRAUMA and chest injury trauma are medical conditions that frequently co‑occur with حادثة سير (traffic accident). In patients involved in a traffic accident, clinicians should be alert to the high likelihood of both facial and chest injuries, as these trauma types commonly arise together in the context of such incidents. Recognizing this association aids in comprehensive assessment, timely diagnosis, and appropriate management of trauma patients following a traffic accident.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2027
retrieved_docs.content: Open wounds (جرح مفتوح) are clinically associated with crushing injuries or internal injuries, indicating that such traumatic mechanisms often result in an open wound presentation. Within the category of open wounds, cut wounds (جرح قطع) represent a specific subtype, meaning that a cut wound is a particular form of open wound. Recognizing that crushing or internal injuries can lead to open wounds, and that cut wounds are a distinct subset of these, aids clinicians in diagnosing the nature of the trauma and selecting appropriate management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2617
retrieved_docs.content: Head injury trauma is frequently observed in the context of a حادث سير (traffic accident). In addition, a حادث سير commonly co‑occurs with وجع في الكتف (shoulder pain). Consequently, head injury trauma, traffic accidents, and shoulder pain are interrelated clinical findings that often appear together in patients involved in vehicular collisions, underscoring the importance of evaluating both cranial and musculoskeletal injuries after such events.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2205
retrieved_docs.content: FACE INJURY TRAUMA and head injury trauma are frequently observed to co‑occur with a traffic accident (حادث سير). The presence of a حادث سير strongly suggests that patients may sustain both facial and cranial injuries, highlighting the need for clinicians to assess for these specific trauma types when evaluating individuals involved in motor vehicle collisions. Recognizing this co‑occurrence improves diagnostic vigilance and guides comprehensive management of injury patterns associated with traffic accidents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 1 -- Community 139
retrieved_docs.content: crash, is associated with extensive tissue damage, reinforcing the link between high‑energy fractures, tissue injury, and neurotoxic effects. Overall, fracture (كسر) integrates late sequelae, specific anatomical fracture types, associated tissue damage, co‑occurring injuries, procedural interventions, and psychosocial classifications, providing a comprehensive framework for patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 264
retrieved_docs.content: pathologies, entrapment syndromes, headache and facial‑nerve disorders, musculoskeletal pathology and diagnostic imaging, thereby supporting comprehensive patient assessment, precise diagnosis and targeted clinical management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2107
retrieved_docs.content: Skull and face fractures, described in Arabic as كسر في قبو القحف, refer to injuries involving breaks in the cranial vault and the facial bones, encompassing a range of traumatic damage to the head and facial structures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2851
retrieved_docs.content: Other non‑traumatic joint disorders are closely associated with joint disease (اعتلال مفصلي), indicating that a range of non‑injury‑related joint conditions fall under the broader category of joint disease. This relationship highlights the importance of recognizing various non‑traumatic joint pathologies as part of the overall spectrum of اعتلال مفصلي for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1973
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to, and may be considered synonymous with, the signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, reflecting the overlap between patient‑reported experiences and observable clinical manifestations in poorly characterized disorders. In this framework, diseases and injuries are placed under the broader category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, indicating that many inadequately defined pathologies present with nonspecific clinical findings that are captured by this umbrella term. Together, these relationships underscore the interconnected nature of clinical presentations (symptoms) and the underlying, often vague, disease or injury processes, highlighting the importance of comprehensive evaluation when managing patients with ill‑defined conditions.
